name: SvelteKit
what_it_does: Full-stack web framework for Svelte — Svelte's answer to Next.js. SvelteKit provides routing, SSR (server-side rendering), SSG (static generation), API routes, form actions, and deployment adapters in a single framework. Svelte's compiler-first approach (no virtual DOM) produces smaller, faster bundles than React. SvelteKit's load functions and actions provide a clean data fetching and form handling pattern. Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and Node.js deployment adapters available.
use_cases: Build full-stack web applications for agent interfaces with Svelte's reactive UI and SvelteKit's server-side rendering
Create agent API backends using SvelteKit's API routes (+server.ts files) alongside the frontend in the same project
Build static documentation sites or agent dashboards with SSG (static generation) and zero runtime overhead
Implement form-based agent interactions using SvelteKit's progressive form actions with JavaScript enhancement
Deploy agent web applications to Cloudflare Workers or Vercel Edge Functions using SvelteKit's edge deployment adapters
not_for: Teams with large React codebases — migration cost from React to Svelte is significant; use Next.js instead
Complex enterprise applications needing extensive React ecosystem (component libraries, state management) — React's ecosystem is much larger
Applications requiring heavy real-time features — SvelteKit's architecture works but other frameworks have more ecosystem support
best_when: You're building a new full-stack web app and prefer Svelte's simpler, compiler-based reactivity over React — especially for performance-conscious or bundle-size-sensitive applications.
avoid_when: Your team knows React, you need the React ecosystem, or you're migrating from a React application — stick with Next.js.

auth.notes: No built-in auth. Use Auth.js (formerly NextAuth) with SvelteKit adapter, Lucia Auth, or custom auth via hooks. Authentication patterns are well-documented in the community.

pricing.notes: MIT open source. Free forever. Deployment costs depend on hosting platform (Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare).

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — framework performance depends on hosting

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Excellent error overlay in development. Build errors from Vite include file paths and line numbers. TypeScript errors in routes are caught at build time. SvelteKit's documentation is among the best in the framework ecosystem.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: SSR is stateless per request by default. Load functions are pure functions — same request produces same data. Form actions handle state transitions explicitly.
known_agent_gotchas: SvelteKit's load function runs on both server and client — agents writing load functions must handle the dual-environment correctly; avoid Node.js-specific APIs that don't run in browser
Form actions only work with HTML forms or fetch POST requests — agents calling form actions from JavaScript must handle the response format (ActionResult) appropriately
SvelteKit's routing uses filesystem conventions (+page.svelte, +page.ts, +layout.svelte) — agents generating routes must follow exact naming conventions or routes won't register
The $app/environment module's browser and dev variables affect code behavior — agents generating code that branches on these must test in both server and client contexts
SvelteKit 2.x introduced breaking changes from 1.x (redirect() and error() imports changed) — verify docs version matches installed version when debugging
Adapter selection (node, static, cloudflare, vercel) affects available APIs — agent code using platform-specific features must match the deployment adapter

security_score_components.security_notes: MIT open source. No built-in auth — security is framework consumer's responsibility. CSRF protection built-in for form actions. XSS protection via Svelte's automatic escaping. env file handling for secrets.
